_comment: Month-level and special period rules. These override tithi + vara rules when active.

Shravana.non_veg_reason: Shravan (sacred month of Shiva) — entire month is considered a period of abstinence. Non-veg strictly avoided by most Hindus, especially in Maharashtra.
Shravana.notes: Mondays in Shravan are especially sacred (Shravan Somvar). Fasting, Shiva abhishek, and Bilva patra offering are traditional.

Kartika.non_veg_reason: Kartik month is the most sacred month of Vishnu — non-veg avoided throughout.
Kartika.notes: Kartik Ekadashi (Prabodhini/Devuthani) is the most important Ekadashi of the year. Tulsi Vivah happens in this month.

pitrupaksha.name: Pitrupaksha / Shraddha Paksha
pitrupaksha.description: The 15-day period in Krishna Paksha of Bhadrapada (or Ashwina in some traditions) dedicated to ancestors. Falls in September–October.
pitrupaksha.non_veg_reason: Pitrupaksha is a period of remembrance for ancestors. Non-veg, alcohol, and festivities are avoided.
pitrupaksha.haircut_reason: Haircut and nailcut are avoided during Pitrupaksha out of respect for ancestors.
rituals: Shraddha puja on the tithi matching the ancestor's death date
Tarpan (offering of water with sesame seeds) daily
Crow feeding (crows are believed to carry ancestor souls)
Pitru Visarjan on Sarva Pitru Amavasya (last day)
pitrupaksha.significance: It is believed that during this period the departed souls come closest to the earth. Performing Shraddha with devotion ensures the ancestors' peace and blessings for the family.

navratri.name: Navratri
navratri.description: 9 nights dedicated to Goddess Durga in her 9 forms. Occurs twice a year — Chaitra Navratri (spring) and Ashwina/Sharada Navratri (autumn, the main one).
navratri.non_veg_reason: Non-veg avoided during Navratri by devotees of Devi. Sattvic diet recommended.
navratri.notes: Many devotees observe a partial or full fast throughout Navratri, eating only once a day (Phalahar) or once every alternate day.

adhika_masa.name: Adhika Masa / Mal Mas / Purushottam Masa
adhika_masa.description: The intercalary (leap) month added to synchronize the lunar calendar with the solar year. Occurs approximately every 3 years.
adhika_masa.significance: Lord Vishnu (Purushottam) is the presiding deity of this month. It is excellent for spiritual activities — fasting, chanting, charitable acts — but inauspicious for auspicious rituals like weddings, upanayana, and griha pravesh.
adhika_masa.notes: Any charity or tapas performed in Adhika Masa yields many times the usual merit.

sankashti_chaturthi.description: Sankashti Chaturthi falls on Krishna Chaturthi (4th day of the waning moon) every month. 'Sankashti' means 'deliverance from troubles'.
sankashti_chaturthi.fast: Complete fast observed till moonrise. After seeing the moon, Ganesha is worshipped and prasad is consumed.
sankashti_chaturthi.significance: Fasting on Sankashti removes obstacles and sorrows. Ganesha grants wisdom, good health, and removes Vignas (obstacles).
sankashti_chaturthi.moonrise_note: The fast must be broken only after sighting the moon. The app should show moonrise time prominently on Sankashti days.

gold_silver_buying.best_overall: Akshaya Tritiya (Shukla Tritiya of Vaishakha) and Dhanteras are the most auspicious days for buying gold and silver.
gold_silver_buying.pushya_nakshatra_note: Any day when Pushya nakshatra falls on a Thursday (Gurupushyamrut Yoga) is extremely auspicious for gold purchase.

food_donation.notes: Annadaan (food donation) on Amavasya and during Pitrupaksha is believed to reach and satisfy the departed ancestors. Donating on Ekadashi pacifies Vishnu and brings peace.
